eli3: Logistic regression models the log-odds of a binary outcome Y ∈ {0,1} as a linear function of predictors: log(p/(1−p)) = β₀ + β₁X₁ + … + βₚXₚ, where p = P(Y=1|X). This ensures predicted probabilities stay in [0,1] without truncation. The logistic (sigmoid) function maps log-odds back to probability: p = 1/(1+e^{−Xβ}).

Coefficients are estimated by Maximum Likelihood — there is no closed-form solution (unlike OLS). MLE maximizes ℓ(β) = Σ[yᵢ log pᵢ + (1−yᵢ)log(1−pᵢ)]. This is solved iteratively with Newton-Raphson or IRLS. The log-likelihood is globally concave (no local optima) for logistic regression, guaranteeing convergence.

The key output is the odds ratio (OR): OR = e^{β̂ⱼ}. An OR of 1.5 means the odds of Y=1 increase by 50% for each unit increase in Xⱼ, holding other predictors constant. ORs are NOT risk ratios — they overestimate RR when the outcome is common (> 10%). For rare outcomes, OR ≈ RR.

whenWhy.title: Binary outcome with linear predictors
whenWhy.body: When the response is binary (disease/no disease, default/no default, click/no click) and you want to model the probability as a function of predictors, logistic regression is the standard parametric approach. It directly models P(Y=1|X) ∈ [0,1] without truncation problems of linear probability models (which can give P > 1 or P < 0).

whenWhy.title: Inference on predictor effects via odds ratios
whenWhy.body: The exponentiated coefficient e^{β̂ⱼ} is the adjusted odds ratio — the multiplicative change in odds for a unit increase in Xⱼ, controlling for other predictors. This is the standard quantity reported in epidemiology and clinical research. Wald 95% CI for OR: exp(β̂ⱼ ± 1.96·SE(β̂ⱼ)). Note: OR is the appropriate effect measure for matched case-control studies where incidence cannot be estimated.

whenWhy.title: Model fit assessment
whenWhy.body: The deviance D = −2ℓ(β̂) replaces SS_res from OLS. Null deviance D₀ = −2ℓ(null model with only intercept). McFadden's pseudo-R² = 1 − ℓ(β̂)/ℓ(null). Hosmer-Lemeshow test checks calibration (agreement between predicted probabilities and observed proportions across deciles). AUC-ROC measures discrimination.

deepDiveIntro: Logistic regression models the probability of the positive class as the logistic function applied to a linear combination of predictors. The log-likelihood is the sum of log probabilities of each observed outcome, and because the Hessian of this function is always negative definite, the likelihood surface is globally concave — there is a unique maximum and no local optima to worry about.

Coefficients are found by iteratively reweighted least squares (IRLS), which is Newton-Raphson applied to the log-likelihood. At each iteration the algorithm solves a weighted least-squares problem using a working response and weight matrix derived from the current probability estimates. This converges in a handful of iterations for well-behaved data.

deepDive.heading: Logit Link and Odds Ratio Interpretation
deepDive.body: The logit link converts probabilities into log-odds so the model can use a linear predictor while still producing valid probabilities. Exponentiating a coefficient gives an odds ratio, which describes the multiplicative change in odds associated with a one-unit change in a predictor. This interpretation is standard in epidemiology and other applied fields, but it should not be confused with a risk ratio.

deepDive.heading: Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT) and Wald Test
deepDive.body: Logistic regression supports several ways to test hypotheses about coefficients. The likelihood ratio test compares overall model fit between nested models, while the Wald test looks at an estimate relative to its own standard error. In practice, the likelihood ratio test is often preferred because it behaves more reliably when coefficients are large or samples are limited.

deepDive.heading: Complete Separation and Convergence Issues
deepDive.body: Logistic regression can fail when the predictors separate the classes too perfectly. In that case, the model keeps pushing coefficients toward infinity because ever-larger values keep improving the likelihood. Penalization, bias-reduced estimation, or simpler models are common fixes for this instability.

deepDive.heading: Model Evaluation
deepDive.body: Logistic models should be judged on both discrimination and calibration. A model may rank cases well while still producing poorly calibrated probabilities, or the reverse. That is why metrics such as AUC, calibration checks, and the Brier score are all useful parts of evaluation.

takeaways: logit(p) = Xβ; coefficient e^{β̂ⱼ} is the adjusted odds ratio for Xⱼ. [1]
MLE via IRLS (Newton-Raphson); log-likelihood is globally concave — no local optima. [1]
OR ≠ RR when prevalence > 10%; OR overestimates RR. OR ≈ RR for rare outcomes. [1]
LRT preferred over Wald test (Hauck-Donner effect: Wald is unreliable for large β̂). [1]
Complete separation: β̂→∞; use Firth's penalized likelihood or Ridge logistic. [1]

edgeCases.q: Can logistic regression handle class imbalance?
edgeCases.a: The MLE for logistic regression is consistent under any class ratio — the slope coefficients are unbiased. However, the intercept β₀ is affected by sampling: in case-control studies (deliberately oversampling cases), β₀ needs correction: β̂₀_corrected = β̂₀ + log(π/(1−π)) − log(ρ/(1−ρ)), where π is the true prevalence and ρ is the case fraction in the sample. Slopes are case-control invariant — a key advantage of logistic regression for retrospective studies. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the linear probability model and why not use it instead?
edgeCases.a: The linear probability model (LPM): E[Y|X] = Xβ, estimated by OLS. It's simple and coefficients have a direct probability interpretation (β̂ⱼ = marginal probability change per unit Xⱼ). Problems: predicted P(Y=1) can be outside [0,1]; heteroscedasticity by construction (Var(Y|X) = p(1−p) depends on X); estimated coefficients are inconsistent for nonlinear P(Y=1|X). For description and A/B testing where X is binary, LPM with robust SE is often acceptable. For prediction or continuous X, logistic regression is better calibrated. [1]

sources.label: Hosmer, Lemeshow & Sturdivant — Applied Logistic Regression (3rd ed.), Ch. 1–5
McCullagh & Nelder — Generalized Linear Models (2nd ed.), Ch. 4
